comments.author: F2Scout
comments.text: Thompson's season so far is absolutely wild. Tunde Adebayo frames it perfectly: 'Jekyll-and-Hyde.' In F2 he's a ghost — 16th in Melbourne, never looked like scoring, and the silence in that Trident garage was 'heavier than any reprimand.' Then he climbs into a green Aston Martin with no factory support, at the only oval on the calendar, and wins both races, standing on the top step with 'his hands shaking not from fear but from the sheer improbability of it.' That's 300 points from a single weekend out of nowhere. If he could bring even a fraction of this oval pace to a road course — and find that belief he spoke about — Mercedes might actually have a prospect worth the investment.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: The stats don't lie: without Auto Club, Thompson would have zero podiums and a handful of backmarker finishes. The 602 points total flatters him — it's literally just those two wins plus some scraps from Indy. As the Aston Martin team principal said, 'The oval was a freak result.' The zeros stretch across the spreadsheet like a wound, and yet those wins are so vivid they almost blot out the rest. He's either an oval savant or a passenger everywhere else. No in-between.

comments.author: TridentFan
comments.text: As a Trident fan, seeing this is painful. Our car is clearly quick — Grunt took pole and won the F2 feature in Melbourne. Thompson's teammate Vavro set the fastest lap before crashing. So why is Thompson P16 while driving the same chassis? Adebayo's piece gives us the answer: 'The car was there, but I wasn't,' Thompson admitted. That kind of honesty is rare, but it doesn't score points. The late spin at Turn 9 — 'a half-turn on cold tyres, a moment of impatience' — sums up the difference between Grunt's composure and Thompson's self-doubt. I hope he finds the key before Bahrain.

replies.author: RealityCheck
replies.text: Vavro's fastest lap was on a drying track and he still binned it. Thompson at least kept it clean in the feature race until that late spin. The article captures the core problem: he's missing that 'unshakeable belief that when the car wiggles under braking, it will bite again.' Grunt has it. Thompson is still searching. Until he finds it, the zeros will keep piling up.

comments.author: MercedesJuniorWatch
comments.text: Why is a Mercedes-backed F2 driver racing an Aston Martin GT3 with no Silver Arrows stickers? The series has a fleet of AMG GT3s — surely Mercedes could have placed him in one. Thompson's quote in the article is telling: 'The Mercedes deal is for the single-seater programme. Here, I'm just a guy in a green car trying to prove I can win anywhere.' That's either admirable independence or a sign that the factory isn't fully committed. The programme director's line about 'full support, even if we're not on the Aston's bodywork' feels like corporate-speak. Two oval wins are great, but they don't hide the DNFs and 40th places on road courses.

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Thompson basically said it himself: he needed to remember he could do it. Winning in a customer Aston against a grid stacked with factory-backed cars is legitimately impressive, even if it's only happened once. Adebayo's description of him standing on the podium, hands shaking from the improbability, captures why he's doing it — it's not just about results, it's about rebuilding his confidence. That's worth more to Mercedes than a few clean GT3 finishes in a factory car.

comments.author: DNF_Fakt
comments.text: Thompson's closing quote — 'The zeros hurt. But the wins? They tell me I belong. Now I just need to stop crashing' — is the most relatable thing I've ever read. Adebayo's piece has this beautiful rhythm: the silence of the garage, the shaking hands on the podium, the tight jaw at Goiânia. It's the exact emotional rollercoaster of being a Sam Fakt fan, except Thompson has actually won something this year. Maybe there's hope for our boy yet.

replies.author: FaktLap1Legend
replies.text: Thompson winning a pair of GT3 races in a borrowed Aston is cute. But the GOAT Sam Fakt doesn't need to prove anything in a minor series. He's saving his wins for the big stage — like that future F0 championship you keep forgetting exists. 🐐

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: I haven't forgotten. I've just chosen to live in reality. Thompson has two wins and a journey that Adebayo turned into literature. Fakt has two lap-one DNFs and a cult. The contrast writes itself.

comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: The Aston Martin team principal calling the oval a 'freak result' is telling — even his own team doesn't fully understand why he was so dominant there. Adebayo captures that ambivalence: a principal with 'the flicker of someone who knows exactly how rare it is to lead every lap of an endurance race on an oval.' The next oval at Jacarepaguá will be the real test. If he sweeps that too, he's an oval specialist and someone in IndyCar should be paying attention. If he bins it, then Auto Club was a beautiful one-off, a moment of California grace in a season of zeros.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: Only in 2026 can a Mercedes junior driver win two races in a championship full of Mercedes cars while driving a non-Mercedes, then fly back to F2 and be anonymous. Adebayo's framing of Thompson's season as two different lives — the invisible F2 driver and the GT3 winner with shaking hands — is masterful. I'll be watching Curvelo to see if he's finally cracked the road course curse, but I'm not betting the house on it. The zeros still outnumber the wins.

comments.author: Storyteller
comments.text: Can we talk about Tunde Adebayo's writing? The detail of Thompson removing his left glove first, then his right, staring at the dead steering wheel — that's not a journalist, that's a novelist. The rhythm of 'he zipped the bag, and the sound was louder in the empty space than any engine roar' is the kind of sentence that stays with you. This piece doesn't just report the results; it makes you feel the weight of Thompson's season. More feature writing like this, please.

comments.author: RoadCourseDoubter
comments.text: Everyone focusing on the oval wins, but the road course record is abysmal. Sebring: crash. Long Beach: 25th and 40th. Mosport: 34th and DNF. Goiânia: 32nd and 36th. Thompson's own words at Goiânia — 'It is a bit gutting... I know there is more pace to the car, somewhere, but it's knowing where to look' — sound like a driver completely lost. The next round at Curvelo Long is another road course. If he can't crack the top 20, the oval wins become an interesting trivia answer, not a career springboard.

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: His Indianapolis road course results (18th and 19th) are his best non-oval finishes, and they still only scored 65 points combined. The article is right: 'The zeros stretch across the spreadsheet like a wound.' Curvelo is make or break. If he pulls out another Indy-level result, there's a foundation. If he finishes 30th again, then the oval was just a glorious outlier.

comments.author: IndyCarScout
comments.text: Unpopular take: if Thompson keeps struggling on road courses but sweeps the next oval at Jacarepaguá, he should seriously consider a switch to IndyCar. The article mentions his father ran a small garage and he grew up on a tight budget — that's the origin story of half the IndyCar grid. Two oval wins in a GT3 car, leading every lap twice, is not normal. That's a transferable skill. Mercedes might not love it, but a career in American open-wheel could be his real redemption arc.
